item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

They were characterised as well-meaning and friendly, but unintelligent and often deficient in their duties, resulting in storms and typhoons.

This line alone was enough for my +1 — simply how much sense this makes really impressed me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is both extremely awesome and extremely sad. Couldn't help but thinking too, it's like the opposite of 96 in a way. Instead of you dying when you look at it, it dies when you look at it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
qntm never disappoints in delivering a solid piece. Short, clever and bringing our attention to the kind of weird problems the Foundation has to face and cannot always solve. Brings to mind the case of tribes in the Amazon, who have to remain uncontacted, due to lack of immunity to diseases from the outside. Also a rather easy introduction to the concept of antimemetic skips.
Edit: Also, the clean version made available by the author was a great help in translating, so thank you for that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My initial hope was that the censored words spelled some hidden info. Still enjoyed it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a great skip, a real hidden gem that deserves more attention. It's extremely well-written, the format screw is clever, the underlying anomaly is interesting, the logic of how it works is so satisfying, and it even manages to be sad and poignant. I will be thinking about these poor guys for a long time. A much belated +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me way too long to get why stuff was redacted.

+1

Edit: reverse 096

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first thoughts were that "wow this is a lot redaction"

My second was "wow, this author is redacting random words"

Upon finishing: "heh, i get it!"

I enjoyed the article, and the idea of these creatures. I assume the fact that we can see these animals, and just not perceive them, means we would still pilot our boats to avoid them, so they are "safe" from ocean and plane traffic.
Additional thoughts:

1) Since the effect persists, there could be more undiscovered groups of this species that persist.
2) they may not be extinct, and the foundation knows this, but are listed as extinct to preserve the species, which is why the word "extinct" is not corrupted, while the date of this decision is.

Question:
1)Would incorrect information, if the incorrect information is about this species, be corrupted? If it said "the species is no more than 250m tall" would this be corrupted?
2) if they took an approach similar to 055, defining what it is not, would this impact the creature? "the species is not less than 500m or more than 1500m tall"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Such a sad story. I think this might be my favorite SCP.
Well-deserved rating for a beautiful mindfuck of an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea of the Foundation's observation being enough to affect the creatures, to the point that it has deleterious effects on the world outside the species population. I don't know if it was your goal, but to me there are definitely some good implications about the way we do field work in sociology or zoology.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
made an art of these amazing creatures…

Very...
...Tall Things

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted them to be able to walk on water :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would it be at all possible to get a copy of that script? I'm running a fantasy tabletop campaign where I'd like to give the players in-game documents with a similar sort of information decay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the quantum level the universe can be modeled as information, this includes the Higgs field…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They weighed no more than 4 tonnes, with most of their mass being "camouflaged" by a very similar adaptation.

The antimemetic effect camouflages most of the mass from reality itself, so the weight it experiences due to gravity is much less than it should be? The laws of physics have a mind that can be screwed with?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought that the blackboxes were really redundant and newbish. Then I got to the end. Fridge Logic much?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have been informed that the genus Cryptomorpha, which I thought I'd invented, is actually pre-existing. This is a mistake on my part, but who knows, maybe these things are massively overgrown jewel beetles :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral until I realized the decay of the article, which I thought was a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, when I first started to get to the last paragraphs, I was thinking to myself, "man, I hate it when people block out information in these things, it's not like it doesn't matter." When I realized that all the blackouts were due to the effect itself, I looked and realized that it was incredible. You made an article with a format screw in which the anomaly isn't contained in the information or article itself but instead is a side-effect of a neutralized anomaly and is the only remnants of the anomaly.

I give you a golden upvote, because geez, this is amazing.

item.title: Perhaps instead of regular black out it could look a little more corrupted?
item.content:encoded: What if instead of just using the full black box ("█"), each character was randomly replaced with one of these: ▀, ▄, ▌, ▐, ▖, ▗, ▘, ▙, ▚, ▛, ▜, ▝, ▞, ▟, █, ▓, ▒, ░; in order to better depict the corruption?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it means that 4 tonnes is their TRUE mass, and most of THAT is camoflagued by this alternative mechanism, so it wouldn't result in massive tsunamis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: Nevermind, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something that hasn't been mentioned in the comments so far:

They weighed no more than 4 tonnes, with most of their mass being "camouflaged" by a very similar adaptation.

This is some weird pocket-dimension kind of thing, maybe nearly as weird as the primary effect. The implications of it could be interesting. I wonder if the effect persisted postmortem. If not, the death of each creature would be accompanied by massive tsunamis as millions of tonnes suddenly fell in the water. The fact that the article doesn't mention any such thing could be explained away by it being written in broad strokes, as per containment. And this effect too evolved naturally, apparently.

I love it. Here's an enthusiastic upvote. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point of this seems to be logical speculation from a single weird premise. I don't think that if "a wizard did it" this article would be remotely as interesting, YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would you say a species of lamb what fires lasers from its eyes probably evolved that way to fend off predators?

That seems to be a pretty good first order hypothesis, yes. That or dominance displays if they also have laser reflective wool. Sheep are grazing herbivores, and weapons seem to be selected for in such organisms for these reasons. What possibly other thing could it be for? I'm confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)
Congrats on reaching 100, qntm! May your Very Tall Things continue to reach new heights! :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not only did that image sprout in my mind as I read it, but I had a dream about them last night. They reproduced via parthenogenesis and had a culture based on oral tradition, which had to be practiced and rehashed constantly to prevent their own camouflage from killing it.

They sang with their many trunks that made meager human beings think their voices were winds, and their shadows clouds. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I've seen that illustration before but it's very close to what I had in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a cool article. Was it by any chance inspired by Salvador Dali paintings? The description reminded me a lot of his painting The Elephants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
qntm, are you planning on editing the entry on a regular basis with a 'redaction level' that increases over time so that, between three and eight years from now, the entire entry is redacted?

That could be cool (even though there is always the edit history).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad, I'd have to say. The excessive censorship was getting a bit iffy, but the mentioning of why at the end sorted that out.
Is their ability to walk on water anomalously-generated? If not, their feet would have to be freakin' huge to distribute four tonnes enough for them to walk on seawater.
Nonetheless, I'll give it my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps you meant to say amnestic? I don't know, maybe you meant it. Correct me If I'm wrong.

I'm fairly sure he meant mnestic. The islanders have a drug which helps them remember things that are hard to remember, like these creatures.

This came off as kind of awkward, it can only be increasing or decreasing, really…perhaps not.

It's not deliberate censorship, but the antimemetic effect. Down-thread the author explains he wrote a program to randomly censor.

Also lot's of censorship in the end, I don't know, maybe a bit unnecessary.

That's kind of the whole point, it is the antimemetic effect of these creatures doing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this drug had mnestic properties,

Perhaps you meant to say amnestic? I don't know, maybe you meant it. Correct me If I'm wrong.

an ██████████ rate of stillbirths.

This came off as kind of awkward, it can only be increasing or decreasing, really…perhaps not.

Also lot's of censorship in the end, I don't know, maybe a bit unnecessary.

Other than that, quite good. In my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had the idea of replacing the specific measurements with more vague ones, like "4 tonnes" becomes "a few tonnes", "30 kilometres" becomes "several dozen kilometres". I'm not sure it would have worked to convey the idea of "broad generalisations only" though, because the idea doesn't really extend to the rest of the description.

It's kind of a problem because I can't explain what the creatures are like without using some specifics.

So, try this idea: ordinarily, all the specifics in the article are unreadable because of antimemetic effects. But the version presented here is the version you'd see if you were on a high dose of mnestics? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's supposed to be accurate, they just can't go really in depth about it. The description isn't too detailed about it either. We only know its size, weight, shape of feet, and a very broad comparison to another animal. We don't know if they have 4 legs or 20 legs, if they have scales or hair, if they have two eyes or more, and a lot of details like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why I come back almost every day, digging for gold in the new article list. Many days are duds; but then, from time to time, you get a nugget like this, and get excited about the Foundation all over again. Kudos!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is just one thing that nags me about this otherwise fun to read article.

Your containment procedure warns to use only broad generalizations to describe the creature with. Yet almost immediately you produce a rather accurate and clear description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When you do a format screw, it's important to make sure the item itself is interesting, and this definitely qualifies. Good animal, good backstory, fun format screw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also just one other comment, I know "there is no canon" and everything, but I prefer continuity to fit together, and this article says they're the largest species to have lived on Earth but we have several larger ones in the database like 169.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the explanation. As for the second question, it didn't mean it had to be related to Halloween, but the some of the other dates seem ordinary and not holiday-relate, except for one other year. Warning: Very long wall of text ahead and I will probably go off-topic.

Here's what I mean.

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-2256's population declined slightly in 1992 and 1993, then dropped sharply from 1994 onwards.

I searched up "1992 Polynesia" and found an article called "French Polynesia, close to the center of the South Pacific Ocean." I found a few references there from 1992, but we'll get to those later. First, this part is particularly interesting.

About 200 years ago, bird communities in moist forest included at least 14 land bird species, including 6 endemic species and 5 regionally endemic species (Pratt et al. 1987, Stattersfield et al. 1998). The islands support a vascular flora of 623 native species including 273 endemic species and one endemic genus (Florence 1987). Notable plant species include tree ferns (Angiopteris evecta) with 9 m wide canopies in Moorean valleys and a scandent cliff-dwelling Metrosideros sp. (Mueller-Dombois & Fosberg 1998).

This is what I found in the references from 1992.

Cowie, R.H. 1992. Evolution and extinction of Partulidae, endemic Pacific island land snails. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London Biological Sciences 335:167-191.

Fosberg, F.R. 1992. Vegetation of the Society Islands. Pacific Science 46:232-250.

Seitre, R. and J. Seitre. 1992. Causes of land-bird extinctions in French Polynesia. Oryx 26:215-222.

Notice how two of them are about extinctions; especially the last one. It's about land-bird extinctions. Remember those 14 land-bird species in the moist forests? They started going extinct around the same time that SCP-2256 started losing popularity. Now, these are land-birds; think ostriches. (Or penguins, but that's unlikely in SCP-2256's environment.) Let's say that SCP-2256 was real and in the same environment as those land-birds; if SCP-2256 didn't eat them or cause their extinction, then what the hell did it? We can't rule out snakes, however. In the summer, snakes will seek cool and moist places. But there's one more thing; in 1962, France had an independent nuclear testing site. Sometime later in 1995, they resumed nuclear testing, after three years, at Fangataufa atoll; the final test being on January 27th, 1996. That same year, two days after the final test, France decided to accede to the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty, and stopped nuclear testing. If that didn't kill or at least off one of those species of land-birds, then I don't know what did, but I'll explain the whole nuclear thing soon. Now, about Fangataufa atoll. As of 2012, it is uninhabited, but not entirely.

Today, Fangataufa serves as a wildlife sanctuary for various species of birds.

A bird sanctuary. Now Fangataufa is in the eastern side of the Tuamotus, a chain of islands and atolls in French Polynesia. The Tuamotus have only 57 bird species. Ten of these are endemic, including the Tuamotu kingfisher, the Tuamotu reed warbler, and the Tuamotu sandpiper. Thirteen species are globally threatened and one is extinct.

Sounds an awful lot like those land-birds, doesn't it? There were 14 species of land-birds, and 14 in the Tuamotus, although one is extinct, and the rest are globally threatened.

So what exactly did happen back there in 1992?


Please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

(I think that's the longest comment I've ever posted in my entire life, and my neck paid the price.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Gigafauna" is just I word I made up to describe a creature which is about a thousand times larger than what we call megafauna.

The date is not significant. If you think it's distracting, I'm happy to change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Gigafauna isn't a proper term per se, but megafauna is. Megafauna essentially means large animals, like elephants and whales. From this, it can be inferred that gigafauna is synonymous with megafauna, or it means even bigger animals. It was likely invented for this article, and it's valid enough.

2. I… think this is coincidental. There's no intentional references to halloween in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have two questions.

First off, could someone please explain what a gigafauna is? From what I'm reading, I assume it's a bigger version of a flower or plant. If that's exactly what it is, then I'm confused to how I have never heard that word before. I apologize if this is a stupid question.

2. Wait, so the last victim died on October 30th, 2006? That's the day before Halloween, that's certain. How, may I ask, is that not suspicious to anyone else? I may be new here, but that's definitely not a coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, +1, this is cool. Awesome, even. It reminds me of an Attenborough documentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-966567#post-2232893

Here's a cool coding thing, if you feel like making Easter Eggs out of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept though how does the memetic field thingy supposed to work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very similar to what I tried to do with SCP-2894 (imply that an anomaly was responsible for a lot of the redaction in the database) but this one conveys that idea more clearly IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe the giant predators sleep for long times. Maybe the giant predators are SCP-169.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is quite cool! It certainly served the article well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel there are two explanations. One is that these beasties were originally relatively small, then, once they adopted their camouflage, they were able to grow enormously large without fear of predators.

The other is that the predators were equally large. And maybe they still exist! Maybe they just have even better camouflage, which is why we can't see them? Perhaps Foundation observation made the creatures more vulnerable to predation, but the Foundation never detected the predators?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first it was an anti-meme, and I groaned a little (another skip we can't know anything about).

Then I finished it and I had a sad. +1, nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I really like about this is the implications of giant predators.

What exactly is big and/or dangerous enough to take on a literally mountain-sized beast? And where is it now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.

I love the animal, and I adore the concept. I'm not 100% sold on blackboxing to represent the antimemetic effect, but only for aesthetic reasons: It's just not how I picture forgetting things as I'm reading. Your method for generating it was brilliant, though.

There are still a few tonal issues. The main one for me was:

The last individual died near Tokelau ██ October 30, 2006.

I think the language is usually something like "the last known individual…" or "the last claimed sighting…". People don't like to speak with certainty about extinct species in the wild, which is especially relevant because these specifically make you forget them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've changed the passages you mentioned.

Because of their enormous height,

In Maikitian mythology SCP-2256 were wandering spirits whom the gods had charged with maintaining the horizon, to ensure that the sky and the water never mixed. They were characterised as well-meaning and friendly

In 2003 observation of SCP-2256 was scaled back

Hope this is an improvement.

We discussed the tag in chat. I wanted to call them Safe because I didn't realise there was a "Neutralized" tag. But it was felt that the lingering antimemetic effects rated a Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely not Neutralized. Part of the anomaly is still very active indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for all of your positive comments.
